\subsection{The $SO(32)$ embedding}

The $SO(32)$ gauge group of Type~I / heterotic string theory
admits the maximal-subgroup chain
$$
SO(32) \supset SU(16) \times U(1)_A
  \supset SU(5) \times SU(3) \times U(1)_A \times U(1)_B\,,
$$
where the fundamental $\mathbf{16}$ of $SU(16)$ decomposes as
$\mathbf{16} = (\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})_{+1}
  \oplus (\mathbf{1},\mathbf{1})_{-15}$,
embedding the five quark flavors times three colors as a single
tensor-product block.  The $U(1)_B$ charges are fixed by
tracelessness: $15 \times (+1) + 1 \times (-15) = 0$.

\paragraph{Complete decomposition of the adjoint.}
The adjoint $\mathbf{496} = \wedge^2(\mathbf{32})$ splits into three
sectors under $SU(16) \times U(1)_A$:
$$
\mathbf{496} \;=\;
  \underbrace{\mathbf{255}\oplus\mathbf{1}}_{q_A = 0}
  \;\oplus\;
  \underbrace{\mathbf{120}}_{q_A = +2}
  \;\oplus\;
  \underbrace{\overline{\mathbf{120}}}_{q_A = -2}\,.
$$
Each piece decomposes further under
$SU(5) \times SU(3) \times U(1)_B$:

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{llcccl}
\toprule
Sector & $SU(5)\times SU(3)$ & dim & $q_A$ & $q_B$ & Origin \\
\midrule
$\mathbf{255}\oplus\mathbf{1}$
 & $(\mathbf{24},\mathbf{8})$ & 192 & 0 & 0
   & $\mathrm{adj}(SU(15))$ \\
 & $\boldsymbol{(24,1)}$ & $\boldsymbol{24}$ & 0 & 0
   & $\mathrm{adj}(SU(15))$ \\
 & $(\mathbf{1},\mathbf{8})$ & 8 & 0 & 0
   & $\mathrm{adj}(SU(15))$ \\
 & $(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})$ & 15 & 0 & $+16$
   & $\mathbf{16}\text{--singlet mixing}$ \\
 & $(\bar{\mathbf{5}},\bar{\mathbf{3}})$ & 15 & 0 & $-16$
   & conjugate \\
 & $2\times(\mathbf{1},\mathbf{1})$ & 2 & 0 & 0
   & $U(1)_{A,B}$ generators \\
\midrule
$\mathbf{120}$
 & $\boldsymbol{(15,\bar{3})}$ & $\boldsymbol{45}$ & $+2$ & $+2$
   & $S^2(\mathbf{5})\otimes\wedge^2(\mathbf{3})$ \\
 & $(\overline{\mathbf{10}},\mathbf{6})$ & 60 & $+2$ & $+2$
   & $\wedge^2(\mathbf{5})\otimes S^2(\mathbf{3})$ \\
 & $(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})$ & 15 & $+2$ & $-14$
   & $(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})\otimes(\mathbf{1},\mathbf{1})$ \\
\midrule
$\overline{\mathbf{120}}$
 & $\boldsymbol{(\overline{15},3)}$ & $\boldsymbol{45}$ & $-2$ & $-2$
   & conjugate \\
 & $(\mathbf{10},\bar{\mathbf{6}})$ & 60 & $-2$ & $-2$
   & conjugate \\
 & $(\bar{\mathbf{5}},\bar{\mathbf{3}})$ & 15 & $-2$ & $+14$
   & conjugate \\
\midrule
 & \textbf{Total} & \textbf{496} & & & \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\noindent
The target states---$(\mathbf{24},\mathbf{1})$ (mesons),
$(\mathbf{15},\bar{\mathbf{3}})$ (diquarks),
$(\overline{\mathbf{15}},\mathbf{3})$ (antidiquarks)---account
for $24 + 45 + 45 = 114$ of the 496 states.  Each appears exactly
once, in a distinct sector of the decomposition.

\paragraph{Why the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$ appears.}
The crucial step is the decomposition of $\wedge^2(\mathbf{16})$.
Since the singlet $(\mathbf{1},\mathbf{1})$ contributes only a
cross-term, the nontrivial content comes from
$\wedge^2[(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})]$.  A standard
identity in multilinear algebra gives
$$
\wedge^2(V \otimes W)
  \;=\; \bigl[S^2(V) \otimes \wedge^2(W)\bigr]
  \;\oplus\; \bigl[\wedge^2(V) \otimes S^2(W)\bigr]
$$
for any finite-dimensional vector spaces $V$, $W$.  The proof is
elementary: an element $T^{(ia)(jb)} = -T^{(jb)(ia)}$ of
$\wedge^2(V\!\otimes\!W)$ decomposes into a part symmetric in
the $V$-indices $(i \leftrightarrow j)$ and antisymmetric in the
$W$-indices $(a \leftrightarrow b)$, plus its complement.  Overall
antisymmetry of the composite index $(ia)$ forces a correlation:
symmetric in one factor implies antisymmetric in the other.

Setting $V = \mathbf{5}$, $W = \mathbf{3}$:
$$
\wedge^2\bigl[(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})\bigr]
  \;=\; (\mathbf{15},\bar{\mathbf{3}})
  \;\oplus\; (\overline{\mathbf{10}},\mathbf{6})\,,
$$
with $S^2(\mathbf{5}) = \mathbf{15}$,
$\wedge^2(\mathbf{3}) = \bar{\mathbf{3}}$,
$\wedge^2(\mathbf{5}) = \overline{\mathbf{10}}$, and
$S^2(\mathbf{3}) = \mathbf{6}$.
The overall antisymmetry of $\wedge^2$ forces the symmetric flavor
representation $\mathbf{15}$ to pair with the antisymmetric color
$\bar{\mathbf{3}}$, and conversely the antisymmetric
$\overline{\mathbf{10}}$ with the symmetric $\mathbf{6}$.
The color assignment is therefore not a choice---it is dictated by
the tensor-product structure of the embedding.

\paragraph{Minimality: $SO(30) \leq SO(32)$.}
The identity above is activated whenever the fundamental
representation of the ambient group contains
$(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})$ as a single tensor-product block, requiring
$\dim \geq 15$ complex $= 30$ real.  The minimal orthogonal group
with this property is $SO(30)$, whose adjoint
$\wedge^2(\mathbf{30})$ has dimension 435 and decomposes identically
in the $\wedge^2$ sector: $(15,\bar{3}) \oplus
(\overline{10},6)$ from $\wedge^2(15)$ and its conjugate.
$SO(32)$ adds two real singlets to the fundamental
($30 + 2 = 32$), which contribute extra
$(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})$ and
$(\bar{\mathbf{5}},\bar{\mathbf{3}})$ cross-terms but do not
affect the $\wedge^2$ sector.

The alternative $SO(16) \times SO(16)$ fails for a structural
reason.  The $\mathbf{16}$ of a single $SO(16)$ factor is too small
to contain the 15-dimensional tensor product
$(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})$ as a block; instead, $SU(5)$ and $SU(3)$
embed as separate summands $(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{1}) \oplus
(\mathbf{1},\mathbf{3})$ inside the $\mathbf{8}$ of
$SU(8) \subset SO(16)$.  With the two factors in a direct sum
rather than a tensor product, $\wedge^2[(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{1})
\oplus (\mathbf{1},\mathbf{3})]$ produces
$\overline{\mathbf{10}} \oplus (\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3}) \oplus
\bar{\mathbf{3}}$ but no $\mathbf{15}$.  If $SU(5)$ and $SU(3)$
sit in different $SO(16)$ factors, no cross-terms exist at all.

\paragraph{The $E_8 \times E_8$ obstruction.}
Every decomposition of $E_8$ under $SU(5)$ produces only exterior
powers of the fundamental: $\mathbf{1}$, $\mathbf{5}$,
$\overline{\mathbf{10}} = \wedge^2(\mathbf{5})$,
$\mathbf{10} = \wedge^2(\bar{\mathbf{5}})$,
$\bar{\mathbf{5}}$, and the adjoint
$\mathbf{24} = \mathbf{5} \otimes \bar{\mathbf{5}} - \mathbf{1}$.
This holds regardless of the intermediate chain
($E_8 \to E_6 \to SO(10) \to SU(5)$,\
$E_8 \to SU(5) \times SU(5)'$, etc.).
The symmetric tensor $\mathbf{15} = S^2(\mathbf{5})$ is
algebraically forbidden.  The definitive obstruction is:
$E_8 \times E_8$ heterotic gives $\wedge^2$, while the composite
spectrum requires $S^2$.

\paragraph{Orientifold projection.}
The 374 states outside the target must be projected out or made
massive.  Wilson lines and standard orbifold projections cannot
separate $(\mathbf{15},\bar{\mathbf{3}})$ from
$(\overline{\mathbf{10}},\mathbf{6})$: both carry identical
$U(1)$ charges $(q_A, q_B) = (+2, +2)$, and any orbifold element
commuting with $SU(5) \times SU(3)$ acts as a scalar on the
irreducible $(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})$ by Schur's lemma, hence
as a scalar on its exterior power.

A mixed orientifold resolves the problem.  In a Type~I
compactification with orientifold planes of opposite type, one
places the $SU(5)$ flavor branes near an $O^-$ plane (Sp-type
projection) and the $SU(3)$ color branes near an $O^+$ plane
(SO-type projection).  The worldsheet parity~$\Omega$ then acts
with opposite signs on the two tensor factors:
\begin{itemize}
\item Sp-type on $SU(5)$: selects symmetric $\mathbf{15}$,
  projects out antisymmetric $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$.
\item SO-type on $SU(3)$: selects antisymmetric $\bar{\mathbf{3}}$,
  projects out symmetric~$\mathbf{6}$.
\end{itemize}
The unwanted $(\overline{\mathbf{10}},\mathbf{6})$ is thereby
doubly projected out---it requires the wrong projection on both
factors simultaneously.  The mechanism is genuinely stringy:
orientifold parity acts on Chan-Paton indices in a way that
distinguishes symmetric from antisymmetric tensor structures,
a distinction invisible to any Lie-algebraic projection.

The remaining unwanted states have straightforward fates:
the $(\mathbf{24},\mathbf{8})$ colored mesons (192 states) and the
bifundamental $(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})$ copies acquire masses
from brane separation on the compact space, of order the
compactification scale; the two $U(1)$ generators become massive
through the Stückelberg mechanism in the usual way.

\paragraph{Open problem.}
An explicit compact\-ification with the mixed $O^-/O^+$ orientifold
structure, correct tadpole cancellation, and the requisite wrapping
numbers (specifically $\prod_i m_a^i = +1$ for the flavor stack
and $\prod_i m_b^i = -1$ for the color stack, ensuring symmetric
and antisymmetric projections respectively) has not been
constructed. The self-referential numerology---five quarks times
three colors gives $15 + 1 = 16$ Chan-Paton factors, forming
$SO(32)$, whose adjoint contains exactly the composites of those
quarks---remains a structural observation awaiting a complete
string vacuum.

\subsection{The $SO(32)$ embedding}

The $SO(32)$ gauge group of Type~I / heterotic string theory
admits the maximal-subgroup chain
$SO(32) \supset SU(16) \times U(1)_A
  \supset SU(5) \times SU(3) \times U(1)_A \times U(1)_B$,
where $\mathbf{16} = (\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})_{+1}
  \oplus (\mathbf{1},\mathbf{1})_{-15}$
embeds the five quark flavors times three colors as a single
tensor-product block.

\paragraph{Complete decomposition.}
The adjoint $\mathbf{496} = \wedge^2(\mathbf{32})$ splits under
$SU(16) \times U(1)_A$ as
$(\mathbf{255}\oplus\mathbf{1})_{0}
  \oplus \mathbf{120}_{+2}
  \oplus \overline{\mathbf{120}}_{-2}$.
The full $SU(5) \times SU(3) \times U(1)_B$ content is:

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{llcccl}
\toprule
Sector & $SU(5)\times SU(3)$ & dim & $q_A$ & $q_B$ & Origin \\
\midrule
$\mathbf{255}\!\oplus\!\mathbf{1}$
 & $(\mathbf{24},\mathbf{8})$ & 192 & 0 & 0
   & $\mathrm{adj}(SU(15))$ \\
 & $\boldsymbol{(24,1)}$ & $\boldsymbol{24}$ & 0 & 0
   & $\mathrm{adj}(SU(15))$ \\
 & $(\mathbf{1},\mathbf{8})$ & 8 & 0 & 0
   & $\mathrm{adj}(SU(15))$ \\
 & $(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})\!+\!(\bar{\mathbf{5}},\bar{\mathbf{3}})$
   & 30 & 0 & $\pm 16$ & cross-terms \\
 & $2\times(\mathbf{1},\mathbf{1})$ & 2 & 0 & 0
   & $U(1)_{A,B}$ \\
\midrule
$\mathbf{120}$
 & $\boldsymbol{(15,\bar{3})}$ & $\boldsymbol{45}$ & $+2$ & $+2$
   & $S^2(\mathbf{5})\otimes\wedge^2(\mathbf{3})$ \\
 & $(\overline{\mathbf{10}},\mathbf{6})$ & 60 & $+2$ & $+2$
   & $\wedge^2(\mathbf{5})\otimes S^2(\mathbf{3})$ \\
 & $(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})$ & 15 & $+2$ & $-14$
   & cross-term \\
\midrule
$\overline{\mathbf{120}}$
 & $\boldsymbol{(\overline{15},3)}$ & $\boldsymbol{45}$ & $-2$ & $-2$
   & conjugate \\
 & $(\mathbf{10},\bar{\mathbf{6}})$ & 60 & $-2$ & $-2$
   & conjugate \\
 & $(\bar{\mathbf{5}},\bar{\mathbf{3}})$ & 15 & $-2$ & $+14$
   & conjugate \\
\midrule
 & \textbf{Total} & \textbf{496} & & & \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\noindent
The target states---$(\mathbf{24},\mathbf{1})$,
$(\mathbf{15},\bar{\mathbf{3}})$,
$(\overline{\mathbf{15}},\mathbf{3})$---account
for $24 + 45 + 45 = 114$ of the 496 states, each appearing
exactly once.

\paragraph{Why the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$ appears.}
The nontrivial content of $\wedge^2(\mathbf{16})$ comes from
$\wedge^2[(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})]$.  A standard identity gives
$$
\wedge^2(V \otimes W)
  \;=\; \bigl[S^2(V) \otimes \wedge^2(W)\bigr]
  \;\oplus\; \bigl[\wedge^2(V) \otimes S^2(W)\bigr]\,.
$$
An antisymmetric tensor $T^{(ia)(jb)} = -T^{(jb)(ia)}$ decomposes
into a part symmetric in $V$-indices and antisymmetric in
$W$-indices, plus its complement; overall antisymmetry of the
composite index forces the correlation.  With $V = \mathbf{5}$,
$W = \mathbf{3}$:
$$
\wedge^2\bigl[(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})\bigr]
  \;=\; (\mathbf{15},\bar{\mathbf{3}})
  \;\oplus\; (\overline{\mathbf{10}},\mathbf{6})\,.
$$
The symmetric flavor $\mathbf{15}$ is forced to pair with
antisymmetric color $\bar{\mathbf{3}}$, and conversely.
The color assignment is not a choice---it is dictated by the
tensor-product structure.

\paragraph{Minimality.}
The identity is activated whenever the fundamental contains
$(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})$ as a tensor-product block, requiring
dimension $\geq 15$ complex $= 30$ real.
$SO(30)$ is the minimal orthogonal group with this property;
$SO(32)$ adds two singlets that do not affect the $\wedge^2$
sector.  $SO(16) \times SO(16)$ fails because no single
$\mathbf{16}$ can hold the 15-dimensional block
$(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})$: $SU(5)$ and $SU(3)$ embed as
separate summands, and $\wedge^2$ of a direct sum produces only
$\overline{\mathbf{10}}$ and $\bar{\mathbf{3}}$, never
$\mathbf{15}$.
The $E_8 \times E_8$ heterotic is likewise blocked: every
decomposition of $E_8$ under $SU(5)$ produces only exterior
powers $\mathbf{1}$, $\mathbf{5}$,
$\overline{\mathbf{10}}$, $\mathbf{10}$, $\bar{\mathbf{5}}$,
$\mathbf{24}$.  The symmetric $\mathbf{15} = S^2(\mathbf{5})$ is
algebraically forbidden.

\paragraph{Orientifold projection.}
Wilson lines and orbifold projections cannot separate
$(\mathbf{15},\bar{\mathbf{3}})$ from
$(\overline{\mathbf{10}},\mathbf{6})$: both carry identical
$U(1)$ charges $(q_A, q_B) = (+2,+2)$, and any orbifold element
commuting with $SU(5) \times SU(3)$ acts as a scalar on the
irreducible $(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})$ by Schur's lemma.
A mixed orientifold resolves this.  In a Type~I
compactification with orientifold planes of opposite type,
the $SU(5)$ flavor branes sit near an $O^-$ plane (Sp-type,
selecting symmetric $\mathbf{15}$) while the $SU(3)$ color
branes sit near an $O^+$ plane (SO-type, selecting antisymmetric
$\bar{\mathbf{3}}$).  Worldsheet parity~$\Omega$ acts with
opposite signs on the two tensor factors, doubly projecting out
the unwanted $(\overline{\mathbf{10}},\mathbf{6})$.
The remaining 192 colored mesons $(\mathbf{24},\mathbf{8})$
and the bifundamental copies acquire masses from brane separation;
the $U(1)$ generators become massive via the St\"uckelberg
mechanism.

\paragraph{Open problem.}
An explicit compactification with the mixed $O^-/O^+$ structure,
correct tadpole cancellation, and the requisite wrapping numbers
($\prod_i m_a^i = +1$ for the flavor stack, $\prod_i m_b^i = -1$
for the color stack) has not been constructed.  The
self-referential numerology---$5 \times 3 + 1 = 16$ Chan-Paton
factors forming $SO(32)$, whose adjoint contains exactly the
composites of those quarks---remains a structural observation
awaiting a complete string vacuum.

The identity is activated whenever the fundamental contains
$(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})$ as a tensor-product block, requiring
dimension $\geq 15$ complex $= 30$ real.
$SO(30)$ is the minimal orthogonal group with this property;
$SO(32)$ adds two singlets that do not affect the $\wedge^2$
sector.  $SO(16) \times SO(16)$ fails because no single
$\mathbf{16}$ can hold the 15-dimensional block
$(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})$: $SU(5)$ and $SU(3)$ embed as
separate summands, and $\wedge^2$ of a direct sum produces only
$\overline{\mathbf{10}}$ and $\bar{\mathbf{3}}$, never
$\mathbf{15}$.
The $E_8 \times E_8$ heterotic is likewise blocked: every
decomposition of $E_8$ under $SU(5)$ produces only exterior
powers $\mathbf{1}$, $\mathbf{5}$,
$\overline{\mathbf{10}}$, $\mathbf{10}$, $\bar{\mathbf{5}}$,
$\mathbf{24}$.  The symmetric $\mathbf{15} = S^2(\mathbf{5})$ is
algebraically forbidden.

\paragraph{Orientifold projection.}
Wilson lines and orbifold projections cannot separate
$(\mathbf{15},\bar{\mathbf{3}})$ from
$(\overline{\mathbf{10}},\mathbf{6})$: both carry identical
$U(1)$ charges $(q_A, q_B) = (+2,+2)$, and any orbifold element
commuting with $SU(5) \times SU(3)$ acts as a scalar on the
irreducible $(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})$ by Schur's lemma.
A mixed orientifold resolves this.  In a Type~I
compactification with orientifold planes of opposite type,
the $SU(5)$ flavor branes sit near an $O^-$ plane (Sp-type,
selecting symmetric $\mathbf{15}$) while the $SU(3)$ color
branes sit near an $O^+$ plane (SO-type, selecting antisymmetric
$\bar{\mathbf{3}}$).  Worldsheet parity~$\Omega$ acts with
opposite signs on the two tensor factors, doubly projecting out
the unwanted $(\overline{\mathbf{10}},\mathbf{6})$.
The remaining 192 colored mesons $(\mathbf{24},\mathbf{8})$
and the bifundamental copies acquire masses from brane separation;
the $U(1)$ generators become massive via the St\"uckelberg
mechanism.
